name: IN THE NEWS
clue: In January 1993 he was elected president of a country 2/3 the size of the one he led 7 months earlier

name: U.S. AUTHORS
clue: In his 1958 essay "Essentials of Spontaneous Prose", he compared a writing technique to a jazz musician's style

name: SCIENCE FICTION
clue: Referring to what's wrongly believed to be a meteorite, "The Falling Star" is the title of Chapter 2 of this 1898 novel

name: ADVERTISING
clue: In the U.S., more money is spent advertising in this medium than any other

name: HISTORIC PROPERTY TRANSACTIONS
clue: On May 15, 1768 France bought this island from Genoa for 2 million livres

name: BUSINESS LOGOS
clue: Early 1900s labels for this beverage brand featured a beaver sitting on a log at the top of a map

name: TV IN THE 2000s
clue: A key scene of this sitcom's 2014 finale after 9 seasons was actually filmed in Season 2

name: U.S. PRESIDENTS
clue: He was sworn in twice as president within 2 years, first by his father & then later by a former U.S. President

name: ARLINGTON NATIONAL CEMETERY
clue: The remains for Arlington's first monument to unknown soldiers mostly came from this battlefield 30 miles away

name: 20th CENTURY AMERICANS
clue: In 1941 & 1942 he served as a photo-muralist for the Department of the Interior

name: NATIONAL PARKS
clue: A biosphere reserve, this Southern national park is the largest in the lower 48 completely within one state

name: THE SUPREME COURT
clue: Warren Harding's 1st appointment to the court set a precedent by making him Chief Justice

name: GEOGRAPHIC EXTREMES
clue: The city of Tromso in this country is home to the world's northernmost university & brewery

name: ROYALTY
clue: He was sixth in line to the Greek throne when he renounced his claim in 1947

name: WORLD THEATER
clue: This 1867 play has a reindeer hunt & a king dwelling in snowy mountains but its title character also spends time in Morocco & Egypt

name: WOMEN
clue: Former Arizona state senator who received a historic federal appointment in 1981

name: PEOPLE IN GOVERNMENT
clue: Now in his job over 17 years, he's the longest-serving pres. appointee other than Supreme Court members
response: Alan Greenspan (Chairman of the Federal Reserve)

name: GARMENTS OF THE WORLD
clue: The custom of hijab, Arabic for "veiling", can include this garment, mentioned by Kipling

name: MYTHS & LEGENDS
clue: This legendary place has been identified as being in Caerleon, Wales & in Winchester, England

name: WORLD CINEMA
clue: The 2007 biopic called "La Môme" in France, meaning "The Kid", was released in the U.S. under this other French title

name: INTERNATIONAL PLAYWRIGHTS
clue: A piece of writing advice from this man who died in 1904 concludes, "Otherwise don't put it there\"

name: SONG STANDARDS
clue: According to Irving Berlin, "They can play a bugle call like you never heard before\"

name: COLONIAL TRADE
clue: The Dutch traded New York to the British for a tiny island producing this, now mainly used at Christmastime

name: NEW ENGLAND
clue: It's the only state in New England that doesn't border the Atlantic Ocean

name: INTERNATIONAL GROUPS
clue: The economic bloc known as the G8 became the G7 in 2014 upon the expulsion of this country

name: THE NOBEL PRIZES
clue: Only one man & one woman have won Nobel Prizes in 2 different categories, with this category in common

name: HISTORIC AMERICANS
clue: General Winfield Scott called him "the very best soldier I ever saw in the field\"

name: 16th CENTURY SCIENTISTS
clue: It is often said of this man that he "stopped the Sun and moved the Earth\"

name: EVENTS OF 2010
clue: A piece of custom-made equipment called the Phoenix played a key role in an October event in this country

name: NOVELISTS
clue: In the April 2000 National Geographic, he wrote an article dispelling myths about the great white shark

name: POPULAR SYMBOLS
clue: Starting in the 1860s, this cartoonist gave us the modern image of Santa Claus

name: THE GRAMMYS
clue: Of the more than 75 competitive categories, this one does not cite the title of any album, song or other project

name: THE OSCARS
clue: The only remake of a U.S. film to win Best Picture; the original was made in the 1920s, the Oscar-winning remake in the 1950s

name: INSECTS
clue: Lampyridae is the scientific name of this family of insects

name: FIRST FAMILIES
clue: The last member of a first family to have been married at the White House
On June 11, 1996 the U.S. Senate voted to name a balcony in his honor

name: FAMOUS MUSICIANS
clue: In the 1950s he composed the scores for Satyajit Ray's brilliant "Apu" trilogy of films

name: HISTORIC BUILDINGS
clue: Begun in the 1070s with stone from Caen, it was meant to dominate both a skyline & the hearts & minds of a conquered populace

name: RECENT HISTORY
clue: In 1986 & again in 2001, people power protests in this nation pushed out male presidents & replaced them with females

name: BY THE NUMBERS
clue: In the 1800s Carl Wunderlich got this number by averaging over a million readings from armpits of 25,000 patients

name: ASIA
clue: 19th century novelist Jose Rizal was a hero of this country's independence movement

name: WORDPLAY
clue: Subtract a letter from the name of a keystroke found in computer commands & you get this violent reaction to social change

name: HISTORICAL SHAKESPEARE
clue: In "Henry VI, Part I" this woman is described as "a holy prophetess new risen up\"

name: IN THE BOOKSTORE
clue: The name of this author dead since 2013 now appears on books written by a former U.S. marshal & a former Apache helicopter pilot

name: POP CULTURE
clue: In 2004 a spokesman announced the "break-up" of this "couple" who had "dated" for 43 years

name: U.S. LAKES
clue: This lake is the remnant of former Lake Bonneville, which existed during the Pleistocene epoch

name: FILMS OF THE '70s
clue: This '71 film opens on the words "To the police officers of San Francisco who gave their lives in the line of duty\"

name: HONORS
clue: To remain "Free to blast and bollock Blairite Britain" Tony Harrison declined this post in 1999

name: HISTORIC TV

name: 19th CENTURY NOVELS
clue: The mention of a new railway section between Rothal & Allahabad in India leads to an argument & then a bet in this novel
response: Around the World in Eighty Days

name: FROM THE GREEK
clue: The word for a song element you won't find in instrumentals comes from the name of this instrument

name: STATE CAPITALS
clue: In 2003 a famous marker on the steps of this city's capitol got an altitude adjustment, being lowered by 3 feet

name: THE PULITZER PRIZES
clue: The first man to win the Pulitzer Prize for his film criticism, he's lobbied for a Pulitzer Prize for the movies

name: THE 1996 ELECTION
clue: Nicknamed for the sport their children play, they were part of the swing vote in 1996

name: MAN-MADE OBJECTS
clue: Around since 1998, it's now roughly the length of a football field & travels at about 5 miles per second

name: WORLD FLAGS
clue: Both Wales & Bhutan have flags bearing one of these mythical creatures

name: 19th CENTURY AMERICA
clue: One of the 2 years in which 3 men served as president of the United States

name: BESTSELLING NOVELISTS

name: PRESIDENTIAL CAMPAIGNS
clue: Though shot in the chest, Teddy Roosevelt gave a 1912 speech saying, "it takes more than that to kill" one of these animals

name: WORLD DRAMA
clue: Sophocles, Euripides & Eugene O'Neill all wrote plays with this woman's name in their titles

name: MILITARY MEN
clue: Last name of the first father & son to be awarded medals of honor, for service in the Civil War & WWII respectively

name: BRITISH KINGS
clue: Bearing Roman numeral I, he subdued Wales & was called the "English Justinian" for his legal reforms

name: MAJOR LEAGUE BASEBALL

response: the Seattle Mariners & the Florida Marlins

name: AMERICANA

name: HISTORIC BUSINESSMEN
clue: Tired of his fragile wares being smashed in transit, this man born in 1730 advocated British turnpike building

name: MOVIE STARS
clue: Matthew McConaughey said, "'Dazed & Confused', the first words I ever said on film were" these

name: LITERARY TITLE CHARACTERS

name: MEDITERRANEAN ISLANDS
clue: This 1,400-square-mile tourist destination's name comes from a Latin word for "greater\"

name: TRANSPORTATION

name: SMALL COUNTRIES
clue: French, Italian & Swiss nationals make up about half of its population of 38,000

name: OFFICIAL STATE SONGS

name: TOURIST ATTRACTIONS

name: CHILDREN'S LITERATURE
clue: The success of this book in 1957 prompted Random House to create its "Beginner Books" series

name: MUSIC IN AMERICA

name: THE CALENDAR
clue: Total days in a year which fall in months with 31 days

name: FAMOUS LAST NAMES
clue: The first woman space shuttle pilot shares this surname with a man on the 1st manned lunar landing 26 years earlier

name: WORD AND PHRASE ORIGINS
clue: Meaning "rapidly", this term began in England, referring to the speed with which the mail was delivered

name: FAMOUS NICKNAMES

response: Che (for Che Guevara, who was born in Argentina)

name: THE 50 STATES
clue: The constitution for the proposed state of Sequoyah served in large part as the basis for this state's constitution

name: POLITICIANS

name: FILM CLASSICS

name: FAMOUS NAMES

name: DEMOCRATS
clue: No. of times the Democratic Party has lost the presidential election since WWII

name: HISTORIC PEOPLE
clue: Venetians called him "Il Milione", man of the million lies

name: FAMOUS WOMEN
clue: Of a famous 1955 event she said, "My only concern was to get home after a hard day's work\"

name: WORD ORIGINS
clue: Like the name of a minor Roman god, this word for a caretaker comes from the Latin for "door\"

name: BIOGRAPHY SUBJECTS
clue: One critic called Peter Martin's book about him "the best biography of the greatest biographer in the English language\"

name: HOLLYWOOD HISTORY
clue: They were the first 2 sisters ever nominated for the same acting Oscar in the same year

name: BUSINESS & INDUSTRY
clue: Cincinnati company that is #1 TV advertiser, spending $½ billion per yr.

name: U.S. GOVERNMENT
clue: He served as Speaker of the House longer than anyone else, almost 17 years

name: BODIES OF WATER
clue: The Latin name of this waterway is Fretum Herculeum

name: SINGERS
clue: Her recording career lasted just 8 years, starting in 1955 with "A Church, A Courtroom And Then Good-Bye\"

name: THE NOBEL PRIZE IN PHYSICS
clue: Barry Barish, who shared the 2017 Prize for detecting gravitational waves, called his award "a win for" this predecessor

name: FROM SCREEN TO STAGE

name: THE CIVIL WAR

clue: Currently, this show is performed by 3 troupes; the Liffey & Lagan Companies on tour & the Shannon Company on B'way

name: AUTHORS

name: THE OSCARS
clue: This Irish-born actor has received 7 "Best Actor" nominations without winning an Oscar

name: OPERA

name: U.S. GOVERNMENT
